message.content:
Panel 1  
[Wide shot. Two teens sitting on a park bench after school.  
JORDAN (16, thoughtful) has a backpack at their feet.  
SAM (17, relaxed) sips from a water bottle. The mood is casual but a bit awkward.]

CAPTION: After health class…

JORDAN: So… today’s lesson. Masturbation.  
JORDAN: I didn’t think they’d actually say the word out loud.

SAM: Yeah, the teacher looked like they wanted to disappear into the floor.

---

Panel 2  
[Closer on Jordan, fidgeting with a zipper pull, a bit embarrassed.]

JORDAN: I mean, I’ve heard about it forever, but it’s still… weird to talk about.  
JORDAN: It’s just… touching yourself, right?

SAM: Pretty much. Masturbation is just when you stimulate your own body—especially your genitals—to feel pleasure.  
SAM: It’s something lots of people do on their own, privately.

---

Panel 3  
[Medium shot of both. Sam is calm, trying to normalize things.]

JORDAN: Is it, like… bad for you? People online say it’ll mess you up.

SAM: That’s myths.  
SAM: As long as you’re not hurting yourself, and it’s not taking over your life, it’s not harmful.  
SAM: Most health experts say it’s a normal part of learning what feels good in your own body.

---

Panel 4  
[Cut to a simple, neutral diagram-style background behind them: just an outline of a human body, no details.]

SAM (off-panel): During puberty, your body’s changing a lot.  
SAM (off-panel): Your hormones go up, you get random sexual feelings and curiosity.  
SAM (off-panel): Masturbation is one way some people explore those feelings on their own.

---

Panel 5  
[Back to the bench. Jordan looks curious but cautious.]

JORDAN: Okay, but… what does it even feel like?  
JORDAN: People talk about it like it’s this massive, life-changing thing.

SAM: It’s different for everyone, but generally it starts as a kind of build‑up of pleasant tension.  
SAM: If you keep going, that can build into a really strong wave of pleasure, then release. That release is an orgasm.

---

Panel 6  
[Sam gestures with their hands, describing the feeling; Jordan listens.]

SAM: Some people feel warm and tingly, some feel their muscles tighten, some just feel a big “whoosh” of relief.  
SAM: Afterwards you might feel relaxed or sleepy.  
SAM: Or sometimes awkward, especially if you’ve heard it’s “wrong” your whole life.

---

Panel 7  
[Jordan looks down, a bit vulnerable.]

JORDAN: Yeah… my family acts like anything sexual is shameful.  
JORDAN: So even thinking about it makes me feel guilty.

SAM: A lot of families and cultures are like that.  
SAM: But feeling sexual things—including masturbating—isn’t the same as being “bad.”  
SAM: It’s your body’s natural response, especially now that you’re going through puberty.

---

Panel 8  
[Jordan looks up, more serious.]

JORDAN: So… it’s normal if I do?  
JORDAN: And also normal if I don’t?

SAM: Exactly.  
SAM: Some people masturbate, some don’t. Some try it and don’t like it.  
SAM: There’s no “you HAVE to” and no “you MUSTN’T EVER.” It’s a personal choice.

---

Panel 9  
[Side view. Sam leans back, stretching.]

SAM: The important stuff is:  
SAM: • Do it in private.  
SAM: • Make sure it’s your own choice, not pressure from someone else.  
SAM: • Don’t use porn or images of anyone who hasn’t clearly agreed to that.  
SAM: And if you follow your own values and keep it safe, it’s not something you need to be ashamed of.

---

Panel 10  
[Jordan looks a bit relieved, half-smiling.]

JORDAN: This is way more helpful than the awkward two minutes in health class.  
JORDAN: They just said “It’s normal” and then changed the slide.

SAM: Honestly, “It’s normal” covers a lot.  
SAM: Your body belongs to you. Finding out what it feels like—at your pace, or not at all—is part of getting to know yourself.

---

Panel 11  
[Wide shot of the two walking away from the bench, backpacks on.]

JORDAN: Thanks for talking about it without making it gross or a joke
